title: Fixing Bitwise Shifts: Incorrect Mask Generation

statement.markdown: Given a binary array `nums` and an integer `k`, determine the total number of sub-arrays that have the highest `k` bits set to 1. The highest `k` bits are determined by performing a bitwise left shift by `k` places. However, the current implementation has a bug.
statement.inputFormat: An array of 0s and 1s, and a non-negative integer `k`.
statement.outputFormat: The total number of sub-arrays with the highest `k` bits set to 1.

hints.text: Review the bitwise shift operation and how it affects the highest bits.

editorial.title: Solving the Bug: Fixing the Mask Generation
editorial.intuition: The correct approach involves iterating over the array and counting sub-arrays with the highest k bits set correctly.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach involves using a nested loop to iterate over all sub-arrays and checking the highest k bits for each.
editorial.betterSolution: Improved solution uses a simple iteration and a bit mask to count the highest k bits.
editorial.optimalSolution: Optimal solution uses a combination of simple iteration, bit mask, and bitwise operations to correctly count sub-arrays.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Proof of correctness: the optimal solution correctly counts sub-arrays with the highest k bits set to 1.
editorial.dryRun: Sample dry run of the optimal solution.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly implementing the bitwise shift operation.
Not properly initializing the bit mask.

functionSignature.python: def highestKBitsSubarrays(self, nums: list[int], k: int) -> int:
